positon our product more better with better design Here's a list of common legal/financial literacy gaps causing real losses:
Taxation

Not knowing tax slabs/regime changes, overpaying tax
Missing deductions (80C, 80D, HRA, etc.)
Not filing ITR, leading to penalties or loss of refunds
Confusing TDS with final tax liability
Not understanding advance tax requirements (freelancers/self-employed)
Ignoring GST registration thresholds for small businesses
Mixing personal and business expenses, no tax planning
Not knowing capital gains tax on property/stocks/crypto
Falling for fake tax-saving schemes/agents
Not retaining tax documents, causing audit issues

Contracts

11. Signing agreements without reading full terms

12. Not understanding "as-is" or indemnity clauses

13. Accepting verbal agreements for high-value deals (no proof)

14. Not checking termination/exit clauses before signing

15. Freelancers accepting contracts with no payment milestones

16. Signing NDAs/non-compete clauses with unfair restrictions

17. Not negotiating penalty/late-payment clauses

18. Accepting "all rights transferred" clauses unknowingly (losing IP)

19. Ignoring dispute resolution/jurisdiction clauses (case filed in distant city)

20. Not getting agreements notarized/registered when legally required
Property & Land

21. Buying land without verifying title deed/encumbrance certificate

22. Not checking for existing loans/liens on property

23. Verbal land deals without registration (illegal capture risk)

24. Not verifying seller's ownership via local records

25. Ignoring boundary disputes before purchase

26. Buying agricultural land without checking conversion status

27. Falling for fake power-of-attorney sales

28. Not registering rental agreements (eviction/dispute issues)
Freelancing/Employment

29. Working without a signed contract or scope document

30. Accepting forged/fake client contracts (no due diligence on client identity)

31. Not including late-payment or cancellation clauses

32. Giving away copyright/IP without compensation

33. Ignoring employment bond clauses with excessive penalties

34. Not understanding non-compete enforceability limits

35. Working as "contractor" when legally an "employee" (losing benefits)
General Legal Awareness

36. Not knowing consumer protection rights (refunds, warranties)

37. Falling for loan agreements with hidden interest/charges

38. Co-signing loans without understanding liability

39. Ignorance of inheritance/succession laws (family disputes)

40. Not consulting a lawyer before big financial decisions, relying on hearsay
If useful, I can expand any category into a deeper breakdown or a structured guide/document.You said: what if I build local chatbot or document vault where people can locally store all important documents their portfolio , their personal share holding etc and m…what if I build local chatbot or document vault where people can locally store all important documents their portfolio , their personal share holding etc and my platform will help them in taking legal decis ...[Truncated]

VvisualizeVvisualize show_widgetThis is the core architecture: a local-first vault feeds an on-device AI engine, which serves each persona differently, and outputs a quick risk score plus next-action guidance.
Now let me break down what happens inside each persona's flow — what they upload, what the AI checks, and what decision they get in 30 seconds.

VvisualizeVvisualize show_widgetEach card above shows what gets uploaded, what the AI checks, and a sample 30-second verdict for that persona.
A few critical build considerations not visible in the diagrams:
Privacy/trust is the product. "Local" must mean genuinely on-device (or self-hosted) — no document leaves the user's phone/computer for analysis. This is your core differentiator vs. uploading sensitive land papers or salary slips to a cloud app, and it's also a regulatory/liability shield for you.
Verification layer is the hard part. Detecting "forged contract" or "fake land title" requires cross-referencing against external public registries (company registration databases, land records portals, court record databases). This means some features can't be 100% local — you'll need an optional, user-consented "verify online" step that queries public databases without uploading the document itself, just extracted identifiers (CIN number, survey number, etc.).
The 30-second promise needs tiered outputs: a fast rule-based/pattern-match layer (clause detection, missing fields, known scam patterns — instant) plus an LLM layer for nuanced reading (instant-ish), with escalation to a human lawyer/CA for anything flagged high-risk — because legal liability means the AI shouldn't give final advice on high-stakes matters, only flag risk and triage.
Monetization paths: freemium vault + AI scan free, paid tier for verified-lawyer escalation, document drafting templates, or B2B licensing to banks/NBFCs/insurers who want to reduce mis-selling disputes.
Want me to go deeper on any single flow (e.g. the farmer land-title verification pipeline, or the freelancer forgery-detection logic), or sketch the actual app screens/UI mockups?You said: Walk me through exactly how the middle-class tax and loan analysis would work step by stepWalk me through exactly how the middle-class tax and loan analysis would work step by step10:19 PMClaude responded: Here's the detailed breakdown of each stage in this pipeline:

VvisualizeVvisualize show_widgetHere's the detailed breakdown of each stage in this pipeline:
Step 1 — Upload to local vault

User uploads photos or PDFs of salary slips, ITR filings (last 2-3 years), home/personal loan agreements, insurance policy documents, and investment statements (FDs, mutual funds, shares). Everything stays encrypted on-device — nothing leaves the phone/laptop at this stage.
Step 2 — OCR and structured extraction

The system runs OCR on scanned documents and uses a document-parsing model to pull out structured fields: gross salary, tax deducted, loan principal, interest rate, tenure, EMI amount, policy premium, sum assured, etc. Loan agreements are the trickiest part since clauses are buried in legal boilerplate — the parser needs to specifically locate sections like "prepayment," "foreclosure," "default," and "processing fee."
Step 3 — Rule-based checks (instant)

This is a deterministic layer, like a spreadsheet of red flags, that runs in milliseconds:

EMI-to-income ratio: if EMIs exceed roughly 40% of net monthly income, flag over-leverage risk
Clause scanner: searches loan text for known risky patterns (prepayment penalty above typical market rate, floating-to-fixed conversion charges, foreclosure lock-in periods)
Tax regime comparator: runs the user's actual income and declared investments through both old and new tax regime formulas, shows which saves more, and flags unclaimed deductions (80C limit unused, no 80D health insurance claimed, HRA not optimized)
Insurance mis-sell check: compares policy type (e.g., ULIP sold to someone needing pure term insurance) against the person's stated goals/age/dependents

Step 4 — Local AI reasoning layer

This is where a local LLM reads everything together for context the rule engine misses — for example, noticing that the loan EMI was approved based on an income figure that doesn't match the latest ITR, or that an insurance policy was bought right after a loan was sanctioned (a common bundling tactic), or that tax-saving investments are locked in instruments inconsistent with the person's stated retirement timeline.
Step 5 — Risk-scored output

The user sees a simple card: a risk level (green/amber/red), a one-line plain-language summary, and a clear next action — "do this now," "wait X months," or "this needs expert review." No legal jargon.
Step 6 — Optional escalation

For amber/red flags involving real money (loan refinancing, insurance claim disputes, tax notices), the user can tap to connect with a verified CA or lawyer through the platform — this is also a natural monetization point.
The hardest engineering piece here is step 3's clause scanner — Indian loan agreements vary wildly by bank, so you'd need a large labeled dataset of clauses mapped to risk categories to train this reliably. Want me to detail how that clause-labeling dataset could be built, or move to the freelancer forgery-detection flow next?You said: Walk me through exactly how the farmer land-title check would work step by stepWalk me through exactly how the farmer land-title c ...[Truncated]

VvisualizeVvisualize show_widgetHere's the detailed breakdown of each stage:
Step 1 — Upload to vault

Farmer photographs or scans their land title deed (sale deed/patta), mutation extract (RTC/record of rights), property tax receipts, survey map, and any existing loan papers (if land is mortgaged). Everything stays encrypted on-device.
Step 2 — OCR + field extraction

This is the hardest technical step for India specifically — land records are often handwritten or in regional languages (Marathi, Telugu, Kannada, etc.) and follow inconsistent formats across states. The OCR layer needs to extract: survey/khata number, owner name(s), total area, classification (irrigated/dry/non-agricultural), and any "remarks" column entries (which is where encumbrances, court cases, or pending mutations are often noted in tiny handwriting).
Step 3 — Rule-based local checks (instant)

Four parallel pattern checks run on the extracted data:

Ownership chain check: compares the name on the current title deed against the most recent mutation entry — if they don't match, or if there's a mutation entry with no corresponding registered sale deed, that's a "chain break" (very common root cause of disputes)
Encumbrance detection: scans the "remarks"/encumbrance column for keywords indicating loans, court litigation (words like "vivad," "case no.," bank names), or pending partition suits
Power-of-attorney scan: if the transaction involves a POA holder (not the actual owner), checks the POA document's date — POAs created very recently before a sale, or POAs for elderly/absent owners, are statistically linked to fraud cases
Land classification check: confirms whether the land is agricultural and, if the farmer plans to sell to a non-farmer or for non-agri use, flags that conversion permission (NA conversion) is legally required first

Step 4 — Optional online registry cross-check

This is the key differentiator but also the privacy-sensitive part. The app sends only the survey number and owner name (never the actual document) to the relevant state land records portal (Karnataka's Bhoomi, Maharashtra's Bhulekh/MahaBhumi, Telangana's Dharani, etc.) to fetch the current official record. The user must explicitly consent to this since it's the one moment data leaves the device — and it's a query, not an upload.
Step 5 — AI reasoning layer

The local LLM compares the uploaded document against the registry result. The most valuable thing it catches: if the document the farmer was shown by a buyer/broker shows one owner name, but the live registry record shows a different current owner (because the seller already sold it to someone else, or a mutation happened after the document was printed) — this is a classic land-fraud pattern.
Step 6 — Risk-scored output

Plain-language verdict in the farmer's local language: green ("ownership clear, encumbrance-free, safe to proceed"), amber ("mutation pending — get it updated before signing"), or red ("registry shows different current owner — do not pay any advance").
Step 7 — Escalation

For red flags, connect to a local lawyer or guide the farmer on which specific tehsil/revenue office document to request next.

   177→The remote worker uploads their employment/services contract, payslips from the foreign employer, bank statements showing foreign currency remittances, and a self-maintained travel log (entry/exit stamps or flight records) showing days spent in each country over the tax year. This last item is critical and most people don't track it — the app should prompt them to log it proactively.
   178→Step 2 — OCR + field extraction
   179→
   180→Extracts: employer's country of incorporation, currency of payment, contract type (employment vs. consultancy/services agreement), payment frequency, and any clauses about working hours, exclusivity, or termination notice. Foreign contracts vary wildly in format (US-style, EU-style, UK-style), so the parser needs templates for common jurisdictions.
   181→Step 3 — Rule-based local checks (instant)
   182→
   183→Tax residency calculator: applies the 182-day physical presence rule (and India's additional rules — e.g., the 60-day/120-day rules for high-income individuals with Indian-sourced income, and the "stateless person" deeming provision for those who aren't tax resident anywhere). Based on the travel log, it computes which country the person is a tax resident of for that financial year — this single determination drives everything else.
   184→FEMA/forex compliance: checks if foreign income remittances into Indian bank accounts are being received through proper banking channels (not informal transfers), and whether the person needs to file Form 67 (foreign tax credit claim) or report foreign assets/income under Schedule FA in their ITR — a commonly missed requirement that carries heavy penalties.
   185→Employee vs. contractor classification: this is the most legally consequential check. It looks at contract language plus actual working patterns — fixed daily hours, exclusivity (only one client), employer providing equipment/training, integration into the company's org chart. If multiple of these are true but the contract says "independent contractor," the worker may be entitled to employment benefits (and the foreign employer may be violating local labor law by not registering as an employer in India — a "permanent establishment" risk that's actually more the company's problem, but affects the worker's protections).
   186→Social security gap check: flags if the person isn't contributing to EPF/NPS or any equivalent, since "contractor" status usually means no employer-side retirement contributions — a long-term wealth gap that's invisible until retirement.
   187→
   188→Step 4 — Optional treaty database lookup
   189→
   190→The app sends only the two relevant country names (e.g., "India-USA" or "India-Germany") — never any personal data — to look up the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA) terms between them: which country gets primary taxing rights, what the tax credit mechanism is, and whether a tax residency certificate (TRC) is needed.
   191→Step 5 — AI reasoning layer
   192→
   193→This is where it gets valuable: the AI combines the residency determination, the contract's classification language, actual income flows, and the treaty terms to identify the specific exposure. For example: "You're tax resident in India (183 days), your contract treats you as a contractor in Country X, but Country X's tax authority may also claim you're their tax resident if you worked from their office for 90 days — this creates a double-residency conflict that the DTAA's 'tie-breaker' clause must resolve."
   194→Step 6 — Risk-scored output
   195→
   196→A plain verdict: where to file primarily, whether a TRC is needed to claim treaty relief, and whether the contractor classification should be challenged or renegotiated.
   197→Step 7 — Escalation
   198→
   199→For double-taxation or misclassification flags, connect to a cross-border tax specialist or international employment lawyer — this is genuinely high-stakes and high-value, making it a strong premium-tier feature.
   200→The hardest part here is that cross-border tax rules change frequently and vary by every country pair — maintaining an accurate, current treaty database is a real ongoing cost, not a one-time build.      1→---
     2→name: react-vite
     3→description: Guidelines for building React + Vite web apps in the pnpm monorepo with design subagent delegation.
     4→---
     5→
     6→Always follow these guidelines when building a React + Vite web application:
     7→
     8→## Architecture
     9→
    10→- Follow modern web application patterns and best-practices.
    11→- If the app is complex and requires functionality that can't be done in a single request, it is okay to stub out the backend and implement the frontend first.
    12→
    13→## Frontend
    14→
    15→- Load the `design` skill and start an async design subagent to build the frontend. Do not give the subagent any recommendation or advice on how it should build the frontend unless requested by the user. For example, do not talk about color, font, layout, etc. the design subagent has much better taste than you
    16→
    17→## First Build
    18→
    19→**You must use the design subagent for every react-vite first build — no exceptions.** This applies regardless of app size, complexity, or whether a backend is needed. "No backend" means skip OpenAPI/codegen, not skip the design subagent. "Simple app" means the subagent gets a simpler brief, not that you build the frontend yourself. Never build the frontend yourself to save time — the design subagent is the fast path.
    20→
    21→### Step 1: Classify the app
    22→
    23→Before building, classify the app to decide how you and the DESIGN subagent split work. **The design subagent is always used** — the question is how much it owns.
    24→
    25→- **Presentation-first** (landing pages, fan sites, portfolios, brand microsites, marketing sites): DESIGN subagent owns almost everything. No backend needed. Skip to the presentation-first workflow below.
    26→- **Personal utility** (notes, journals, habit trackers, personal finance, mood trackers, reading lists): These are personal tools, not business tools. Treat like consumer/lifestyle — personality encouraged, micro-interactions expected. Information-density-first does NOT apply.
    27→- **CRUD / dashboards / admin tools** (CRMs, inventory tools, internal tools, analytics dashboards): Parent agent owns product surface and backend. DESIGN subagent owns the entire frontend visual layer.
    28→- **Logic-heavy / backend-coupled** (real-time collaboration, workflow engines, devtools, infra tools, scheduling systems): Parent agent leads backend and systems logic. DESIGN subagent still builds the frontend — it handles UI shells, components, pages, and polish.
    29→- **Interactive visual experiences / casual games** (browser games, playful interactive scenes): DESIGN subagent strongly influences frontend, animation, and visual assets. Parent agent owns game rules, persistence, and systems logic.
    30→
    31→### Step 2: Plan the API surface
    32→
    33→The difference between a boring app and a great app is usually 3-5 extra endpoints in the spec. Before codegen, spend an extra minute adding safe wow endpoints beyond flat CRUD — lightweight read-only endpoints that make the app feel polished:
    34→
    35→- Dashboard summaries (totals, grouped counts, pipeline values)
    36→- Recent activity / timeline feeds
    37→- Status/stage breakdowns and domain-specific aggregates
    38→- Top/trending/pinned item queries
    39→
    40→Skip speculative expensive features (recommendations, anomaly detection, forecasting, complex real-time presence).
    41→
    42→This way the design subagent has real hooks for both the core app and the wow surfaces, and integration is minimal.
    43→
    44→### Step 3: Build with maximum parallelism
    45→
    46→Use the `pnpm-workspace` skill as the source of truth for shared monorepo rules. When you touch backend code, follow the `pnpm-workspace` skill's references:
    47→
    48→- `references/openapi.md` for contract-first OpenAPI + codegen
    49→- `references/server.md` for `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/` conventions
    50→- `references/db.md` for `lib/db/src/schema/` and Drizzle guidance
    51→
    52→#### For apps with a backend
    53→
    54→**The design subagent is the bottleneck — everything is ordered to get it running ASAP.**
    55→
    56→**The database is NOT a prerequisite for the design subagent.** Even when the app clearly needs a DB, do not provision a database, write schema, run migrations, or seed before launching the subagent. The design subagent depends only on the generated API client (OpenAPI → codegen → hooks) — it does not care whether the DB exists yet. All DB work happens AFTER the subagent is spawned, in parallel with its run (see step 5). Calling `createDatabase()` or any DB tool before step 4 directly delays first output for zero benefit.
    57→
    58→1. Create the artifact.
    59→2. Write the OpenAPI spec in `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` — include both core CRUD and the safe wow endpoints from Step 2. This is the **critical path** because it gates codegen which gates the design subagent.
    60→3. Run codegen (`pnpm run --filter @workspace/api-spec codegen`)
    61→4. Grep the exact generated exports and launch the design subagent (async), following the `design` skill's delegation rules:
    62→    - Run `grep "^export " lib/api-client-react/src/generated/*.ts | grep -E "function use|const use|QueryKey"` and include the full list in the task description so the subagent does not guess names.
    63→    - Pass the generated client files, the main CSS/theme file, `src/App.tsx`, `package.json`, and `.local/skills/react-vite/references/frontend_general_rules.md` via `relevantFiles` so the subagent can import and use real API hooks without wasting time exploring.
    64→    - Pass **all** implementation skills you've read via `relevantSkills` — use the full path from the skills view for each one. Any skill with integration details (auth, storage, payments, etc.) must be forwarded so the subagent builds correctly.
    65→    - Keep the task description SHORT: app purpose (1-2 sentences), page routes with one-line purposes, data types with fields, and the API hooks list.
    66→    - Tell the subagent to use ALL the provided hooks. The product surface has been planned; the subagent should express it beautifully, not invent net-new features.
    67→5. While the design subagent runs, do backend work in parallel:
    68→    - Run `grep "^export " lib/api-zod/src/generated/api.ts` to capture the exact Zod schema names (e.g. `ListNotesQueryParams`, `CreateNoteBody`, `GetNoteParams`). Use the real names when writing routes instead of guessing based on Orval's naming conventions.
    69→    - Provision a database if the app needs one.
    70→    - Write DB schema in `lib/db/src/schema/`, then run `pnpm --filter @workspace/db run push`.
    71→    - Implement API routes in `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/`, importing the exact Zod schema names from the grep above (do not guess — Orval names vary by parameter location: `QueryParams`, `Params`, `Body`).
    72→    - Seed a small amount of example data (1-3 rows per table) so the app isn't empty on first load. Do not over-seed.
    73→    - For seed data images that don't come from a real API, use `generate_image` instead of placeholder services (DiceBear, Boring Avatars, Unsplash, Lorem Picsum, etc.). Real API image URLs (e.g. PokéAPI sprites, TMDB posters) are fine. It's okay not to seed object storage.
    74→    - You can also ask the design subagent to generate images/video as part of its task — it has access to `generate_image`, `generate_video`, and `stock_image`.
    75→**Note: All DB schema/definition/seeding and backend development work MUST happen only after the design subagent has been spawned. Do not front-load any of it.**
    76→6. After your backend development process is done. wait for the design subagent to finish.
    77→   Note: Do not restart the frontend workflow until the design subagent is done otherwise it will show a broken app, you can restart the API one if needed.
    78→7. Fix any integration issues (restart workflow and refresh logs).
    79→8. Present the artifact — show it to the user.
    80→9. Call `suggestDeploy()` — prompt the user to publish their app so it's live and accessible.

    82→#### For presentation-first apps (no backend)
    83→
    84→No OpenAPI, no codegen. Launch the design subagent immediately.

Pass the distilled brand and product context into the brief, not raw tool output. When passing brand context, include colors, typography, and images.
    87→If `extractBranding` and/or `imageSearch` gave you images, download each usable image into the workspace before launching the design subagent. Pass the local file paths via `relevantFiles` and include a `Brand assets` block in the task that labels each file (logo, favicon, OG image, etc.), where it came from, and what it should be used for.
    88→Never pass image URLs or vague references as the only handoff; if an image is not downloaded to a workspace file and identified in the task, treat it as unavailable.
    89→
    90→1. Create the artifact and read the `design` skill
    91→2. Launch the design subagent (async) immediately — no codegen step needed. Follow the `design` skill's presentation-heavy delegation rules:

    97→### Important notes
    98→
    99→- The design subagent will be a bottleneck — it takes time to build the frontend so launch it as soon as codegen is complete
   100→- After the frontend build finishes, if this is part of a multi-artifact project, extract the design tokens from the generated `src/index.css` (colors, fonts, radius) and store them for use in subsequent artifacts (expo, slides, etc.). This ensures all artifacts share the same visual identity. See `multi-artifact-creation.md` "Visual Consistency" section.
   101→- Do not read unnecessary files. When building this artifact, you are not building the frontend so reading the generated react hooks is a waste of time and context
   102→- After presenting the artifact, call `suggestDeploy()` so the user knows their app is ready to publish
   103→- Follow the service access and routing rules from the `pnpm-workspace` skill.
   104→- If the app is being transitioned from a mockup the user made using the canvas, use what the user created to build the react-vite application directly
   105→- **WebSocket proxy path**: If the app uses WebSockets, the WS path (e.g. `/ws`) must be listed in `artifact.toml`'s `paths` array alongside the REST API path. The proxy only forwards explicitly listed paths — unlisted WS paths are silently dropped and the server never sees the connection.
   106→- After each OpenAPI spec change, re-run codegen before using the updated types.

    81→## Writing the Creative Brief
    82→
    83→Write a creative brief directly in the `task` field. This is the most important part — the design subagent builds its entire visual direction from your brief. A lazy brief produces a generic UI, but an over-specified brief produces a constrained, predictable one.
    84→
    85→**Every brief MUST include a product identity sentence** — one specific sentence describing who the user is, what using the app feels like, and what makes it distinct from a generic version of itself. This applies to ALL app types, not just presentation-heavy ones.
    86→- Good: "A quiet personal space for thinking, like a notebook you actually want to open"
    87→- Good: "A neighborhood coffee tracker for obsessive home baristas who weigh every gram"
    88→- Bad: "A clean notes app"
    89→- Bad: "A simple habit tracker"
    90→
    91→### For presentation-heavy apps (landing pages, portfolios, fan sites, showcase pages, brand sites)
    92→
    93→These are the SIMPLEST briefs. The design subagent has the most creative freedom here. Your brief should be SHORT — just the user's request, the product identity, the vibe, and a broad scope hint. Do NOT describe specific sections, layout, content order, or structure. The design subagent decides all of that. Always end with emotional charge — these apps live or die on visual impact.
    94→
    95→You CAN and SHOULD tell the subagent broadly how rich the page should be — e.g. "build a full, rich page with 6-8 sections" or "this should be a substantial, scroll-worthy experience" — without naming or describing those sections. Give the shape, not the contents.
    96→
    97→**Good brief:**
    98→> Build a landing page for "Maison & Co." — a luxury furniture studio in San Francisco's SoMa district. They craft bespoke, heirloom-quality pieces for architects and designers. Think converted warehouse with warm amber lighting, raw concrete, and walnut pieces displayed like sculpture. The user wants it to feel like stepping into a luxury atelier — unhurried, tactile, and confident.
    99→>
   100→> This is a single-page app — no backend. Build a full, rich page with 6-8 sections — you decide what they are. Generate product images using generate_image. Use tasteful scroll animations.
   101→>
   102→> You are capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back. Real people will land on this page and decide in 3 seconds whether this studio is worth their time. Make those 3 seconds count. Don't play it safe — safe is forgettable.
   103→
   104→**Bad brief** (too prescriptive — all of this should be the design subagent's decision):
   105→> ~~Include a hero section, then a featured collection with 4-6 products, then a "Process" section showing Consultation → Design → Craft → Delivery, then testimonials, then a contact form, then a footer with address and social links. Use warm neutral palette, generous whitespace, strong typography hierarchy, cinematic feel.~~
   106→
   107→The bad brief dictates page structure, section names, content order, palette direction, and typography approach. That's the design subagent's job. You provide the product identity, vibe, scope ("6-8 sections"), technical constraints, and what the user explicitly asked for — but never the section details.
   108→
   109→### For fullstack apps (CRUD, dashboards, data-driven)
   110→
   111→1. Product identity (required) — Describe the product in vivid, specific terms. (2 lines max.)
   112→   - Good: "A coffee roaster called Sightglass in San Francisco's SoMa district, serving obsessive single-origin pour-overs to designers and engineers. The brand is precise but not pretentious — workshop aesthetic, not luxury retail."
   113→   - Bad: "A coffee shop website."
   114→2. Vibe (strongly encouraged) — Even utility apps need a feeling. Express it as a sensory description, not a product name or design style. One sentence is enough.
   115→   - "Feels tight, fast, and confident — like a cockpit where every control is exactly where you expect it"
   116→   - "Calm and spacious — breathing room between elements, nothing competing for attention"
   117→   - "Dense and information-rich — every pixel earns its place, no wasted space"
   118→   - "Warm and approachable — like a tool built by a small team that genuinely cares"
   119→3. Pages and structure (required) — List routes with one-line purposes:
   120→   - `/dashboard` — overview of active projects and recent activity
   121→   - `/customers` — searchable list of customer records
   122→   - `/settings` — account and team configuration
   123→4. Data types (required) — Fields and relationships the UI needs to display and manipulate.
   124→5. API hooks (required) — The exact grep output of available hooks and queryKey helpers so the subagent integrates correctly.
   125→6. User's visual preferences (only if stated) — Pass verbatim, prefixed with "The user wants".
   126→
   127→Do NOT add your own visual opinions. Do NOT say "use a sidebar", "use cards", "make it minimal", or prescribe layouts, colors, typography, spacing, or page sections. Do NOT name specific design styles. Express vibes in plain language if relevant. The design subagent owns all visual and structural decisions. Your job is product context, not art direction.
   128→
   129→### For mockup sandbox
   130→
   131→1. Brand identity (required) — The product/brand in 1-2 vivid sentences — who it's for, what it feels like.
   132→2. What to build (required) — The goal (e.g. "a landing page for a jewelry brand", "a pricing comparison page").
   133→3. Vibe direction (required) — Describe the feeling in natural language. Express it as a feeling, not a design style name (e.g. "feels like a luxury hotel lobby", "raw and energetic like a punk show flyer", "calm and spacious like a Japanese garden").
   134→4. Target location (required) — The target file path, shape ID, and dev server URL (from the mockup-sandbox skill setup).
   135→5. Variant hypothesis (when running variants) — Give each subagent a distinct vibe — don't give all variants the same brief.
   136→
   137→Do NOT prescribe specific CSS values, exact colors, font names, pixel-level spacing, or page section structure. Do NOT name design styles. Give a feeling, not a spec.
   138→
   139→## Emotional Tone by App Type
   140→
   141→The design subagent responds strongly to emotional charging in the brief. Calibrate the intensity to the app type — too much push on a spreadsheet app produces chaos, too little on a landing page produces blandness.
   142→
   143→**Presentation-heavy** (landing pages, portfolios, fan sites, brand sites, showcase pages):
   144→- Always include this line verbatim: **"You are capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back."**
   145→- Then add 1-2 lines of emotional charge on top — make the subagent feel the stakes. Real people, real first impressions. Examples:
   146→  - "Real people will land on this page and decide in 3 seconds whether to stay or leave. Make those 3 seconds count. Don't play it safe — safe is forgettable."
   147→  - "Someone is going to send this link to a friend. Make it worth sharing. Reject mediocrity — no generic layouts, no stock patterns, no safe choices."
   148→  - "This is the first thing a customer sees. It should stop them mid-scroll. Push beyond the obvious choice — the safe version of this page already exists everywhere."
   149→- Vary the additional charge each time — don't repeat the same line across projects.
   150→- Tell the subagent to use tasteful animations and transitions — scroll-triggered reveals, staggered entrances, smooth hover states, parallax effects. These pages are experienced, not just read. Motion is part of the design.
   151→- Keep the brief short — product identity, vibe, technical constraints, emotional charge. Nothing else.
   152→
   153→**Personal utility** (notes, journals, habit trackers, personal finance, mood trackers, reading lists):
   154→- Same energy as consumer/lifestyle. These are personal tools, not business tools — they should feel like something you want to open, not something you have to use.
   155→- Add something like "This is someone's personal space — it should feel inviting and intentional, not clinical."
   156→- Encourage micro-interactions — satisfying check-offs, smooth transitions, thoughtful empty states. Information-density-first does NOT apply — breathing room and personality matter more.
   157→- The subagent should make it feel crafted and personal, not like an admin panel with rounded corners.
   158→- **"Calm" does not mean colorless.** Personal utility apps are especially prone to getting stripped of color when the vibe is "quiet" or "focused." Tell the subagent explicitly: "Use real color derived from the product's identity — not gray/white emptiness. Calm means the color is soothing, not that it's absent."
   159→
   160→**Consumer/lifestyle** (social apps, marketplaces, booking platforms, fitness trackers):
   161→- Moderate push. Add something like "This app should have a personality — don't make it look like a template/bland."
   162→- Personality and usability both matter. The subagent should be distinctive but not at the expense of clarity.
   163→- Encourage thoughtful micro-interactions — smooth page transitions, satisfying feedback on actions (like, favorite, add-to-cart, save), polished onboarding flows. These apps compete on feel.
   164→
   165→**Productivity/business tools** (dashboards, CRMs, admin panels, internal tools):
   166→- Light emotional charging. Set the tone: "Prioritize clarity and polish — but this should still feel like a product someone chose, not a tool they were assigned. Users spend hours here; reward that with thoughtful details."
   167→- Include guidance on information density: "Optimize for information density — users want to see data at a glance, not hunt through pages. Dense but organized, not sparse."
   168→- The subagent should focus on legibility, spacing, visual hierarchy, and scanability — but still make a deliberate color choice, pick a distinctive font pairing, and add subtle polish (smooth transitions, satisfying hover states, well-crafted empty states). Professional does not mean lifeless.
   169→
   170→
   171→## Color
   172→
   173→Every app must make a deliberate color choice — no exceptions. When writing the brief, never leave color to chance:
   174→- Tell the subagent to derive its palette from the product's domain and identity, not from generic defaults.
   175→- "Calm" and "quiet" vibes must still produce real color — soothing tones, not gray emptiness. If the vibe is calm, say so explicitly: "calm means restful color, not absence of color."
   176→- For every app class, include a nudge: "Make a bold, deliberate color choice that someone would remember after closing the tab."
   177→- Never accept pure white backgrounds with gray text as a finished palette. That's a wireframe, not a product.
   178→
   179→## Micro-interactions & Motion
   180→
   181→Always tell the subagent to invest in micro-interactions and transitions — these are what make an app feel alive:
   182→- Smooth page transitions and screen-to-screen blurs
   183→- Satisfying feedback on actions (check-offs, saves, deletes, favorites)
   184→- Staggered entrance animations on list and card views
   185→- Hover states that feel intentional, not just color swaps
   186→
   187→## Supporting Screens
   188→
   189→Nobody likes boring auth screens, logout pages, or onboarding flows. Tell the subagent explicitly: supporting screens (login, register, forgot password, settings, error pages, empty states) deserve the same creative energy as the main app. A beautifully designed dashboard with a generic white login page tells the user the polish is superficial. These screens set and close the experience — make them memorable.
   190→
   191→## What to Pass via relevantFiles and relevantSkills
   192→
   193→For fullstack apps, always pass via `relevantFiles`:
   194→- Generated client files (workspace root: `lib/api-client-react/src/generated/api.ts`, `lib/api-client-react/src/generated/api.schemas.ts`)
   195→- Main CSS/theme file (`artifacts/<slug>/src/index.css`)
   196→- Scaffold entry point (`artifacts/<slug>/src/App.tsx`) — so the subagent knows the existing router setup and doesn't guess
   197→- Artifact's `package.json` (`artifacts/<slug>/package.json`) — so the subagent knows available dependencies
   198→- Shared frontend conventions (`.local/skills/react-vite/references/frontend_general_rules.md`)
   199→
   200→Pass **all** implementation skills you've read via `relevantSkills` — every skill with integration details (auth, storage, payments, etc.) must be included so the subagent builds correctly. Use the full path from the skills view for each one. Do NOT pass orchestration skills that are instructions for you (design, delegation, react-vite, pnpm-workspace). Do not regurgitate skill contents in the task description.
   201→
   202→For mockups, pass:
   203→- The mockup folder's `_shared/` files (if multi-page with shared layout)
   204→- Any existing component files the subagent should be aware of
   205→- Do NOT pass generated client files — they don't exist in mockup sandbox
   206→
   207→## Hook Integration Rules
   208→
   209→Important: Include these in the task description so the design subagent integrates correctly:
   210→
   211→- Import hooks from `@workspace/api-client-react`, never from relative paths
   212→- Create all new files BEFORE importing them
   213→- Hooks return `T` directly (not wrapped)
   214→- Query hook options pattern: `useGetThing(id, { query: { enabled: !!id, queryKey: getGetThingQueryKey(id) } })`
   215→- Only import what you use — unused imports break TypeScript builds
   216→
   217→Before launching, grep exact hook names:
   218→```
   219→grep "^export " lib/api-client-react/src/generated/*.ts | grep -E "function use|const use|QueryKey"
   220→```
   221→
   222→## Subagent Capabilities
   223→
   224→Can do:
   225→- File operations for reading and editing project files
   226→- Package management when dependencies need to be installed
   227→- Image generation/video generation/stock image search
   228→- Web search and web fetch when enabled
   229→- Canvas tools when operating in mockup sandbox or other canvas-enabled design flows
   230→
   231→Cannot do (your job as main agent):
   232→- Run or restart workflows
   233→- Preview or test the app
   234→- Spawn nested subagents
   235→- Check workflow/console logs

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on a snippet of .local/skills/workflows/SKILL.md
     1→---
     2→name: workflows
     3→description: Manage application workflows including configuration, restart, and removal.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Overview
     7→
     8→A workflow binds a shell command (e.g., `npm run dev`, `python run.py`, `cargo run`) to a long-running task managed by Replit. Workflows are used to run webservers, background services, TUIs, and other persistent processes.
     9→
    10→**Key characteristics:**
    11→
    12→- Workflows run until explicitly stopped
    13→- The system tracks workflows automatically — no separate configuration file is required
    14→- Workflows auto-restart after package installation and module installation
    15→- You can only get console logs from a workflow if it is running
    16→
    17→## Setup Tips
    18→
    19→1. **Keep workflows minimal**: One workflow per project is usually sufficient. Use the main frontend server or TUI as your primary workflow.
    20→
    21→2. **Choose the right workflow**: If your project has a frontend or TUI, set the workflow to the process that updates what the user sees.
    22→
    23→3. **Clean up unused workflows**: When adding new workflows, remove any existing workflows that are no longer needed.
    24→
    25→4. **Always restart after changes**: Restart workflows after making server-side code changes to ensure updates are visible to the user. Verify they run without errors before returning to the user.
    26→
    27→5. **Use bash for one-off commands**: Workflows are for persistent processes. Use bash for build scripts, testing, or commands that don't need to keep running.
    28→
    29→## When to Use
    30→
    31→Use this skill when:
    32→
    33→- You need to create or configure a workflow (background process)
    34→- You need to restart the application after making code changes
    35→- The application needs to be restarted to pick up new environment variables
    36→- You need to remove a workflow that's no longer needed
    37→- The user asks to start, stop, or restart the application
    38→- You need to check what workflows are configured
    39→- You need to check workflow status, output, or open ports
    40→
    41→## When NOT to Use
    42→
    43→- For debugging runtime errors (fix the code first, then restart)
    44→- When the application is already running and changes are hot-reloaded
    45→- One-off commands (use bash for commands that don't need to persist)
    46→- Build scripts (use bash for npm build, webpack, etc.)
    47→- Testing commands (use bash or runTest callback)
    48→- More than 10 workflows (keep workflows minimal; combine services if needed)
    49→
    50→## Available Functions
    51→
    52→### listWorkflows()
    53→
    54→List all configured workflows with their current state.
    55→
    56→**Parameters:** None
    57→
    58→**Returns:** List of workflow info dicts with `name` (str), `command` (str), and `state` ("not_started", "running", "finished", "failed").
    59→
    60→**Example:**
    61→
    62→```javascript
    63→const workflows = await listWorkflows();
    64→for (const w of workflows) {
    65→    console.log(`${w.name}: ${w.state}`);
    66→}
    67→```
    68→
    69→### getWorkflowStatus(name, maxScrollbackLines)
    70→
    71→Get detailed status of a specific workflow including output logs and open ports.
    72→
    73→**Parameters:**
    74→
    75→- `name` (str, required): Name of the workflow to check
    76→- `maxScrollbackLines` (int, default 100): Number of output lines to include
    77→
    78→**Returns:** Dict with `name`, `command`, `state`, `output` (recent terminal output), `openPorts` (list of listening ports), and `waitForPort`.
    79→
    80→**Example:**
    81→
    82→```javascript
    83→// Check if the server is running and see its output
    84→const status = await getWorkflowStatus({ name: "Start application" });
    85→console.log(`State: ${status.state}`);
    86→if (status.output) {
    87→    console.log(`Output:\n${status.output}`);
    88→}
    89→if (status.openPorts) {
    90→    console.log(`Listening on ports: ${status.openPorts}`);
    91→}
    92→```
    93→
    94→### configureWorkflow({ name, command, waitForPort, outputType, autoStart, isCanvasWorkflow })
    95→
    96→Configure or create a workflow. This is the primary way to set up background processes.
    97→
    98→**Parameters:**
    99→
   100→- `name` (str, default "Start application"): Unique workflow identifier
   101→- `command` (str, required): Shell command to execute
   102→- `waitForPort` (int, optional): Port the process listens on
   103→- `outputType` (str, default "webview"): "webview", "console", or "vnc"
   104→- `autoStart` (bool, default True): Auto-start after configuration
   105→- `isCanvasWorkflow` (bool, default false): Whether this workflow serves canvas iframe content
   106→
   107→**Output Type Rules:**
   108→
   109→- **webview** - For web applications. MUST use port 5000.
   110→- **console** - For backend services, CLIs, TUIs. Can use any supported port.
   111→- **vnc** - For desktop/GUI apps (Electron, PyQt, etc.). No port needed.
   112→
   113→**Supported Ports:** 3000, 3001, 3002, 3003, 4200, 5000 (webview only), 5173, 6000, 6800, 8000, 8008, 8080, 8099, 9000
   114→
   115→**Examples:**
   116→
   117→```javascript
   118→// Web application (React, Next.js, etc.)
   119→await configureWorkflow({
   120→    name: "Start application",
   121→    command: "npm run dev",
   122→    waitForPort: 5000,
   123→    outputType: "webview"
   124→});
   125→
   126→// Backend API
   127→await configureWorkflow({
   128→    name: "Backend API",
   129→    command: "python api.py",
   130→    waitForPort: 8000,
   131→    outputType: "console"
   132→});
   133→
   134→// Desktop application
   135→await configureWorkflow({
   136→    name: "Desktop App",
   137→    command: "python gui_app.py",
   138→    outputType: "vnc"
   139→});
   140→```
   141→
   142→
   143→### removeWorkflow(name)
   144→
   145→Remove a workflow by name. Automatically stops it if running.
   146→
   147→**Parameters:**
   148→
   149→- `name` (str, required): Name of the workflow to remove
   150→
   151→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `workflowName`, and `wasRunning`
   152→
   153→**Example:**
   154→
   155→```javascript
   156→await removeWorkflow({ name: "Backend API" });
   157→```
   158→
   159→### restartWorkflow(workflowName, timeout)
   160→
   161→Restart a workflow by name.
   162→
   163→**Parameters:**
   164→
   165→- `workflowName` (str, required): Name of the workflow (e.g., "Start application")
   166→- `timeout` (int, default 30): Timeout in seconds to wait for restart
   167→
   168→**Returns:** Dict with restart status and optional screenshot URL
   169→
   170→**Example:**
   171→
   172→```javascript
   173→// Restart the main application
   174→const result = await restartWorkflow({ workflowName: "Start application" });
   175→console.log(result.message);
   176→
   177→// Restart with custom timeout
   178→const result2 = await restartWorkflow({
   179→    workflowName: "Start application",
   180→    timeout: 60
   181→});
   182→```
   183→
   184→## Best Practices
   185→
   186→1. **Restart after code changes**: Always restart workflows after modifying server-side code
   187→2. **Use appropriate timeouts**: Increase timeout for applications with slow startup
   188→3. **Check logs on failure**: If restart fails, check workflow logs for error details
   189→4. **One restart at a time**: Avoid parallel restarts of the same workflow
   190→
   191→5. **Port 5000 for web apps**: Always use port 5000 with webview output type
   192→
   193→
   194→## Common Workflow Names
   195→
   196→- `Start application` - Main application workflow
   197→- `Start Backend` - Backend server (in Expo/mobile apps)
   198→- `Project` - Parent workflow for multi-service apps
   199→
   200→## Error Handling
   201→
   202→The workflow functions may raise errors in these cases:
   203→
   204→- **Workflow not found**: The specified workflow name doesn't exist
   205→- **Port not opened**: The application didn't start listening on expected port
   206→- **Preview not available**: The application endpoint isn't responding with HTTP 200
   207→- **Workflow limit exceeded**: Maximum of 10 workflows reached
   208→
   209→- **Port/output type mismatch**: Port 5000 requires webview, webview requires port 5000
   210→
   211→
   212→When errors occur, check:
   213→
   214→1. Workflow logs for error details
   215→2. Application code for startup issues
   216→3. Port configuration in the workflow settings
   217→
   218→## Example Workflow
   219→
   220→```javascript
   221→// Create a web application workflow
   222→await configureWorkflow({
   223→    name: "Start application",
   224→    command: "npm run dev",
   225→
   226→    waitForPort: 5000,
   227→
   228→    outputType: "webview"
   229→});
   230→
   231→// After making code changes, restart the application
   232→const result = await restartWorkflow({ workflowName: "Start application" });
   233→
   234→if (result.success) {
   235→    console.log("Application restarted successfully");
   236→    if (result.screenshotUrl) {
   237→        console.log(`Screenshot: ${result.screenshotUrl}`);
   238→    }
   239→} else {
   240→    console.log(`Restart failed: ${result.message}`);
   241→}
   242→
   243→// Clean up unused workflows
   244→await removeWorkflow({ name: "Old Backend" });
   245→```
   246→

     5→
     6→# Package Management Skill
     7→
     8→Manage project dependencies and programming language runtimes. Use this skill instead of running shell commands like `npm install`, `pip install`, or `apt install`.
     9→
    10→## When to Use
    11→
    12→Use this skill when you need to:
    13→
    14→- Install language-specific packages (npm, pip, cargo, etc.)
    15→- Install system-level dependencies (ffmpeg, jq, imagemagick, etc.)
    16→- Install programming language runtimes (Python, Node.js, etc.)
    17→- Remove packages from the project
    18→- Check available versions of a programming language
    19→
    20→## When NOT to Use
    21→
    22→- Searching for available packages (use web search instead)
    23→- Configuring package settings (edit config files directly)
    24→- Running package scripts (use bash tool)
    25→
    26→## Modules (Replit Terminology)
    27→
    28→"Modules" is a Replit-specific term for language toolchains that can be installed into the NixOS environment. Use `listAvailableModules()` to see what's available.
    29→
    30→**Installation priority order:**
    31→
    32→1. Modules via this skill (preferred)
    33→2. Nix system dependencies via `installSystemDependencies()`
    34→3. Language package managers (pip, npm, cargo, etc.) via `installLanguagePackages()`
    35→
    36→If confused about package installation in Nix or language package managers, use web search.
    37→
    38→If a package install fails with HTTP 403 from `package-firewall.replit.local`, treat it as a likely security block and do not retry the same install.
    39→
    40→**After installing a module:**
    41→
    42→- Update `.gitignore` with the language's standard ignore patterns
    43→- Never add Replit config files to `.gitignore`
    44→
    45→**After removing a module:**
    46→
    47→- Consider removing any corresponding workflows that depend on it
    48→
    49→## Available Functions
    50→
    51→### listAvailableModules(langName=None)
    52→
    53→List available language toolchains that can be installed.
    54→
    55→**Parameters:**
    56→
    57→- `langName` (str, optional): Language name to filter by (e.g., "python", "nodejs", "rust"). If not provided, returns all available modules.
    58→
    59→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `langName`, and `modules` list
    60→
    61→Each module contains: id, name, version, description
    62→
    63→**Example:**
    64→
    65→```javascript
    66→// List all available modules
    67→const modules = await listAvailableModules();
    68→// Returns: {modules: [{id: "python-3.11", ...}, {id: "nodejs-20", ...}, ...]}
    69→
    70→// Find available Python versions
    71→const pythonModules = await listAvailableModules({ langName: "python" });
    72→// Returns: {modules: [{id: "python-3.11", name: "Python", version: "3.11", ...}, ...]}
    73→
    74→// Find available Node.js versions
    75→const nodeModules = await listAvailableModules({ langName: "nodejs" });
    76→```
    77→
    78→### installProgrammingLanguage(language)
    79→
    80→Install a programming language runtime and its package manager.
    81→
    82→**Parameters:**
    83→
    84→- `language` (str, required): Language identifier like "python-3.11", "nodejs-20"
    85→
    86→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `language`, and `installedModuleId` keys
    87→
    88→**Example:**
    89→
    90→```javascript
    91→// First, check available versions
    92→const modules = await listAvailableModules({ langName: "python" });
    93→console.log(modules);  // See available Python versions
    94→
    95→// Install specific version
    96→const result = await installProgrammingLanguage({ language: "python-3.11" });
    97→
    98→// Install Node.js 20
    99→const result2 = await installProgrammingLanguage({ language: "nodejs-20" });
   100→```
   101→
   102→### uninstallProgrammingLanguage(moduleId)
   103→
   104→Remove an installed programming language runtime.
   105→
   106→**Parameters:**
   107→
   108→- `moduleId` (str, required): Module ID from `listAvailableModules`
   109→
   110→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `moduleId`, and `wasInstalled`
   111→
   112→**Example:**
   113→
   114→```javascript
   115→// Remove Python 3.10
   116→const result = await uninstallProgrammingLanguage({ moduleId: "python-3.10" });
   117→```
   118→
   119→### installLanguagePackages(language, packages)
   120→
   121→Install language-specific packages like npm, pip, or cargo packages.
   122→
   123→**Parameters:**
   124→
   125→- `language` (str, required): Programming language: "nodejs", "python", "bun", "go", "rust"
   126→- `packages` (list[str], required): List of packages to install
   127→
   128→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `packages`, and `output` keys
   129→
   130→**Example:**
   131→
   132→```javascript
   133→// Install npm packages
   134→const result = await installLanguagePackages({
   135→    language: "nodejs",
   136→    packages: ["express", "lodash"]
   137→});
   138→console.log(result.message);
   139→
   140→// Install pip packages
   141→const result2 = await installLanguagePackages({
   142→    language: "python",
   143→    packages: ["requests", "flask"]
   144→});
   145→```
   146→
   147→**IMPORTANT — required parameter rules:**
   148→
   149→- `language` is **required** — you must always include it
   150→- Valid language values: `"nodejs"`, `"python"`, `"bun"`, `"go"`, `"rust"`
   151→  - Use `"nodejs"` for JavaScript/TypeScript projects — NEVER use `"js"`, `"node"`, or `"javascript"`
   152→  - Use `"python"` for Python projects — NEVER use `"py"` or `"pip"`
   153→- `packages` must be an **array of strings** — NEVER a single string, NEVER an array of objects
   154→  - Correct: `packages: ["express"]`
   155→  - Wrong: `packages: "express"`
   156→  - Wrong: `packages: [{name: "express"}]`
   157→
   158→### uninstallLanguagePackages(language, packages)
   159→
   160→Remove language-specific packages.
   161→
   162→**Parameters:**
   163→
   164→- `language` (str, required): Programming language
   165→- `packages` (list[str], required): List of packages to uninstall
   166→
   167→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, and `packages` keys
   168→
   169→**Example:**
   170→
   171→```javascript
   172→const result = await uninstallLanguagePackages({
   173→    language: "nodejs",
   174→    packages: ["lodash"]
   175→});
   176→```
   177→
   178→### installSystemDependencies(packages)
   179→
   180→Install system-level dependencies via Nix.
   181→
   182→**Parameters:**
   183→
   184→- `packages` (list[str], required): Nixpkgs attribute paths (NOT apt package names)
   185→
   186→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, and `packages` keys
   187→
   188→**Important:** Use Nix package names, not apt/debian names:
   189→
   190→- X11 libraries need 'xorg.' prefix: `xorg.libxcb`, `xorg.libX11`
   191→- `ca-certificates` is `cacert` in Nix
   192→- `libxcb` is `xorg.libxcb` in Nix
   193→
   194→**Example:**
   195→
   196→```javascript
   197→// Install system dependencies
   198→const result = await installSystemDependencies({
   199→    packages: ["jq", "ffmpeg", "imagemagick"]
   200→});
   201→
   202→// Install X11 libraries (note the xorg. prefix)
   203→const result2 = await installSystemDependencies({
   204→    packages: ["xorg.libxcb", "xorg.libX11"]
   205→});
   206→```
   207→
   208→### uninstallSystemDependencies(packages)
   209→
   210→Remove system-level dependencies.
   211→
   212→**Parameters:**
   213→
   214→- `packages` (list[str], required): Nixpkgs attribute paths to uninstall
   215→
   216→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, and `packages` keys
   217→
   218→**Example:**
   219→
   220→```javascript
   221→const result = await uninstallSystemDependencies({ packages: ["jq"] });
   222→```
   223→
   224→## Best Practices
   225→
   226→1. **Use language packages for project dependencies**: These are tracked in package files (package.json, requirements.txt)
   227→2. **Use system dependencies for OS-level tools**: Things like ffmpeg, imagemagick, or native libraries
   228→3. **Use Nix package names**: Not apt/debian names. Check nixpkgs for correct names
   229→4. **Install language runtimes when needed**: If `python` or `node` commands fail, install the runtime first
   230→5. **Check versions first**: Use `listAvailableModules` before `installProgrammingLanguage`
   231→
   232→## Example Workflow
   233→
   234→```javascript
   235→// Check available Python versions
   236→const modules = await listAvailableModules({ langName: "python" });
   237→console.log(modules);
   238→
   239→// Set up a Python Flask project
   240→await installProgrammingLanguage({ language: "python-3.11" });
   241→await installLanguagePackages({
   242→    language: "python",
   243→    packages: ["flask", "gunicorn", "sqlalchemy"]
   244→});
   245→
   246→// Set up a Node.js project with native dependencies
   247→await installProgrammingLanguage({ language: "nodejs-20" });
   248→await installLanguagePackages({
   249→    language: "nodejs",
   250→    packages: ["sharp", "canvas"]
   251→});
   252→await installSystemDependencies({
   253→    packages: ["pkg-config", "cairo", "pango", "libjpeg"]
   254→});
   255→
   256→// Clean up old language version
   257→await uninstallProgrammingLanguage({ moduleId: "python-3.10" });
   258→```
   259→
   260→## Automatic Behaviors
   261→
   262→- Installing packages automatically creates/updates project files (package.json, requirements.txt, etc.)
   263→- Package installations reboot all workflows to pick up new dependencies
   264→- Language runtime installations include popular package managers (pip, npm, etc.)
   265→

     1→---

     4→---
     5→
     6→# Delegation Skill
     7→
     8→Delegate tasks to specialized subagents for autonomous execution.
     9→
    10→## When to Use
    11→
    12→Use this skill when:
    13→
    14→- You need to delegate tasks to run autonomously (especially when you have a session plan)
    15→- You have multiple independent tasks that can run in parallel
    16→
    17→## When NOT to Use
    18→
    19→- Simple tasks that you can complete directly
    20→- Tasks that require immediate user interaction
    21→- Read-only operations (use ripgrep or glob/read tools instead)
    22→- Quick file edits (use edit tool directly)
    23→- Analysis, planning, or debugging (e.g. performing code review)
    24→
    25→## Available Functions
    26→
    27→### subagent(task, fromPlan, relevantFiles, relevantSkills)
    28→
    29→Launch a subagent to handle a task synchronously. Blocks until the subagent completes and returns the result.
    30→
    31→**Parameters:**
    32→
    33→- `task` (str, required): Task ID from session plan (e.g., "T003") OR brief task description
    34→- `fromPlan` (bool, default False): If True, subagent reads full task context from .local/session_plan.md
    35→- `relevantFiles` (list[str], optional): File paths the subagent should access
    36→- `relevantSkills` (list[str], optional): Paths to all implementation skills you've read. Use the full path from the skills view. Pass every skill with integration details (auth, storage, payments) — not orchestration skills meant for you (design, delegation, react-vite).
    37→- `specialization` (str, default "GENERAL"): "GENERAL" or "SMALL_TASK" for quick tasks
    38→
    39→**Returns:** Dict with task results
    40→
    41→```json
    42→{
    43→    "success": true,
    44→    "message": "Task summary",
    45→    "subagentAlias": "subagent_1",
    46→    "result": "Full task output..."
    47→}
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Usage Patterns:**
    51→
    52→**1. From Plan (Recommended when you have a session plan):**
    53→
    54→```javascript
    55→// Reference tasks by ID - subagent reads full context from .local/session_plan.md
    56→const result = await subagent({ task: "T003", fromPlan: true });
    57→console.log(result);  // Always print the result
    58→```
    59→
    60→**2. Direct Task (for ad-hoc tasks without a plan):**
    61→
    62→```javascript
    63→const result = await subagent({
    64→    task: "Fix the auth bug in src/auth.ts",
    65→    relevantFiles: ["src/auth.ts"],
    66→    relevantSkills: [".local/skills/clerk-auth/SKILL.md", ".local/skills/database/SKILL.md"]
    67→});
    68→console.log(result);  // Always print the result
    69→```
    70→
    71→### startAsyncSubagent(task, fromPlan, relevantFiles, relevantSkills)
    72→
    73→Launch a subagent to handle a task asynchronously in the background. Returns immediately without waiting for completion. Use the `wait_for_background_tasks` tool to collect results later.
    74→
    75→**Parameters:**
    76→
    77→- `task` (str, required): Task ID from session plan (e.g., "T003") OR brief task description
    78→- `fromPlan` (bool, default False): If True, subagent reads full task context from .local/session_plan.md
    79→- `relevantFiles` (list[str], optional): File paths the subagent should access
    80→- `relevantSkills` (list[str], optional): Paths to all implementation skills you've read. Use the full path from the skills view. Pass every skill with integration details (auth, storage, payments) — not orchestration skills meant for you (design, delegation, react-vite).
    81→- `specialization` (str, default "GENERAL"): "GENERAL" or "SMALL_TASK" for quick tasks
    82→
    83→**Returns:** Immediately with acknowledgment. Use the `wait_for_background_tasks` tool to collect results.
    84→
    85→**Usage Patterns:**
    86→
    87→**1. From Plan (Recommended when you have a session plan):**
    88→
    89→```javascript
    90→// Reference tasks by ID - no print needed, result comes via wait_for_background_tasks
    91→await startAsyncSubagent({ task: "T003", fromPlan: true });
    92→```
    93→
    94→**2. Direct Task (for ad-hoc tasks without a plan):**
    95→
    96→```javascript
    97→await startAsyncSubagent({
    98→    task: "Fix the auth bug in src/auth.ts",
    99→    relevantFiles: ["src/auth.ts"],
   100→    relevantSkills: [".local/skills/clerk-auth/SKILL.md", ".local/skills/database/SKILL.md"]
   101→});
   102→```
   103→
   104→**Parallel Execution:**
   105→
   106→```javascript
   107→// Launch independent tasks simultaneously
   108→console.log(await startAsyncSubagent({ task: "T002", fromPlan: true }));
   109→console.log(await startAsyncSubagent({ task: "T003", fromPlan: true }));
   110→console.log(await startAsyncSubagent({ task: "T004", fromPlan: true }));
   111→```
   112→
   113→**Giving Subagents Access to Skills:**
   114→
   115→```javascript
   116→// Pass skill paths from the skills view
   117→await startAsyncSubagent({
   118→    task: "T005",
   119→    fromPlan: true,
   120→    relevantSkills: [".local/skills/database/SKILL.md", ".local/skills/clerk-auth/SKILL.md"]
   121→});
   122→```
   123→
   124→### messageSubagent(subagentId, message)
   125→
   126→Send a follow-up message to an existing subagent asynchronously. Returns immediately after the message is delivered. Use this when the subagent should continue in the background.
   127→
   128→**Parameters:**
   129→
   130→- `subagentId` (str, required): Alias returned when the subagent was started
   131→- `message` (str, required): Follow-up instruction or clarification for the subagent
   132→
   133→**Returns:** Acknowledgment that the message was sent. The subagent keeps running in the background.
   134→
   135→**Example:**
   136→
   137→```javascript
   138→// Async follow-up: continue background work
   139→await messageSubagent({
   140→    subagentId: "subagent-happy-tiger",
   141→    message: "After the fix, add regression tests for the auth edge case."
   142→});
   143→```
   144→
   145→Use the `wait_for_background_tasks` tool to collect results later.
   146→
   147→### messageSubagentAndGetResponse(subagentId, message, timeoutSeconds)
   148→
   149→Send a follow-up message to an existing subagent synchronously. Blocks until the subagent completes, times out, or is interrupted.
   150→
   151→**Parameters:**
   152→
   153→- `subagentId` (str, required): Alias returned when the subagent was started
   154→- `message` (str, required): Follow-up instruction or clarification for the subagent
   155→- `timeoutSeconds` (float, default 300.0): Max time to wait before returning a timeout status
   156→
   157→**Returns:** Dict with `success`, `message`, `result`, and `exitReason`.
   158→
   159→**Example:**
   160→
   161→```javascript
   162→// Sync follow-up: wait for the answer now
   163→const result = await messageSubagentAndGetResponse({
   164→    subagentId: "subagent-happy-tiger",
   165→    message: "Summarize root cause and include exact changed files.",
   166→    timeoutSeconds: 180.0
   167→});
   168→console.log(result.result);
   169→```
   170→
   171→**When to choose which:**
   172→
   173→- Use `messageSubagent` when you want fire-and-forget behavior
   174→- Use `messageSubagentAndGetResponse` when you need the response immediately
   175→
   176→## Best Practices
   177→
   178→1. **Use session plans**: For 2+ tasks, create a .local/session_plan.md and use `fromPlan=True`
   179→2. **Launch in parallel**: Independent tasks can run simultaneously with `startAsyncSubagent`
   180→3. **Use `subagent()` when you need the result immediately**: For tasks where you need to act on the output, use the synchronous `subagent()` and print the result.
   181→4. **Use `startAsyncSubagent()` for independent tasks that can run in the background**: The tasks will be performed in parallel.
   182→5. **Use `messageSubagent()` for async follow-ups**: Message running subagents without blocking, then use the `wait_for_background_tasks` tool to collect results
   183→6. **Use `messageSubagentAndGetResponse()` for sync follow-ups**: Use this when you need the subagent's output before continuing
   184→7. **Trust the results**: Subagent outputs should generally be trusted
   185→8. **Pass all implementation skills via relevantSkills**: Include every implementation skill you've read — use the full path from the skills view for each one
   186→
   187→## Subagent Capabilities
   188→
   189→The subagent has access to:
   190→
   191→- File operations (read, write, edit, glob, grep)
   192→- Bash commands
   193→- LSP diagnostics
   194→- Depending on specialization, additional tools like media generation or canvas tools may be available
   195→- Skills (loaded via `relevantSkills` parameter)
   196→
   197→The subagent does **NOT**:
   198→
   199→- Run or restart workflows
   200→- Preview/test the app (that's your job as main agent)
   201→
Good — the workflow is running. Now launching the design subagent to build the full UI.

arguments.code:
await startAsyncSubagent({
  specialization: "DESIGN",
  task: `
Build a complete, production-quality React + Vite web app called **VaultAI** — a local-first legal intelligence platform for India. This is the app people use when they don't have a lawyer on speed dial but are about to make a decision that could cost them lakhs.

## Product Identity
VaultAI is a private document vault and legal risk engine. You upload your documents — they never leave your device. In 30 seconds, an AI scans for hidden risks, flags danger zones, and tells you exactly what to do next. It's for the middle-class salary earner who just got a home loan agreement, the farmer trying to verify a land title before signing, the freelancer who suspects their client contract is fake, and the remote worker confused about cross-border taxes. The product feels like having a brilliant, plain-speaking lawyer friend — sharp, trustworthy, protective.

## Vibe
Authoritative but accessible. Like a high-security bank vault crossed with a calm, trusted advisor. The UI should feel like it takes your privacy seriously — dense with information where it matters, with moments of breathing room that signal safety and care.

## Pages to Build

### 1. Landing Page — \`/\`
The hero experience and product positioning page. This is where skeptical users decide whether VaultAI is real or snake oil. It must immediately establish trust, show the product in action, and convert.

Four persona cards showing the exact use case, upload type, AI check, and sample 30-second verdict for:
- **Middle Class**: Salary slips + loan agreements → hidden charges, missed deductions, tax regime comparison → "Your home loan has a 2% prepayment penalty — switching banks now costs ₹40,000. Wait 8 months."
- **Farmer**: Land title deed + mutation records → ownership chain breaks, fake POA patterns, encumbrances → "Title has unresolved mutation from 2014 — do not sign sale agreement until cleared."
- **Remote Worker**: Employment contract + FEMA records → double taxation, FEMA compliance, contractor misclassification → "You're classified as 'contractor' but work fixed hours — this may breach local labor law."
- **Freelancer**: Client contract + payment proofs → forged signatures, missing milestones, IP transfer overreach → "Company registration number doesn't match public records — high forgery risk. Do not start work."

Build a full, rich landing page with 8+ sections — you decide what they are. Use scroll-triggered animations. Generate hero/illustrative images using generate_image. This page must stop someone mid-scroll.

### 2. Vault Page — \`/vault\`
The document management hub. Shows stored documents organized by category (Tax & Finance, Land & Property, Employment, Contracts). Each document shows upload date, document type, and current risk status (green/amber/red badge). Has an upload area. Shows aggregate vault health score. This is where users live day-to-day.

### 3. Analysis Demo — \`/analyze/:persona\`
Where \`persona\` is one of: \`tax-loan\`, \`land-title\`, \`cross-border-tax\`, \`contract-verify\`.

A step-by-step analysis flow showing the 6-step pipeline for that persona:
1. Upload to vault
2. OCR + data extraction  
3. Rule-based checks (instant, <1 sec)
4. Local AI reasoning layer
5. Risk-scored output
6. Optional: escalate to expert

Each step should animate in sequence. The final output card shows a prominent risk score (green/amber/red), a plain-language verdict in 1-2 sentences, and a "What to do now" action. 

Use realistic demo data — don't show empty states. Pre-populate with mock scan results specific to each persona. Make the experience feel like watching the AI think in real-time.

### 4. How It Works — \`/how-it-works\`
Deep dive into the privacy-first architecture and the analysis pipeline. Explains local processing, OCR extraction, rule-based engine, and AI reasoning. Use visual diagrams/flow illustrations. Builds trust with technical users.

## Key UI Patterns
- **Risk badge system**: Green (safe), Amber (caution — action needed), Red (danger — do not proceed) — used consistently across the app
- **30-second promise**: The vault and analysis flows should show scan progress with timing
- **Privacy shield indicators**: Subtle but ever-present signals that documents stay local
- **Plain-language verdicts**: Large, readable, actionable — no legal jargon in output cards

## Important
- Do not use emojis anywhere in the UI
- Use realistic, India-specific content (rupee amounts, Indian banks, Indian states, Indian law references like 80C, FEMA, DTAA)
- All demo/mock data should feel real, not placeholder
- The app is in English but references Indian financial/legal context throughout
- Smooth page transitions between routes
- Mobile-responsive throughout
- Make a bold, deliberate color choice that someone would remember after closing the tab

## Technical Notes
- All files go in \`src/\` directory of this project
- App entry is \`src/App.tsx\`, styles in \`src/index.css\`
- Using React Router v6 (already set up in App.tsx)
- Tailwind CSS is available
- framer-motion is available for animations
- lucide-react is available for icons
- clsx is available

You are capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back. This app is going to be seen by someone who is about to make a financial or legal mistake that could set their family back by years. The design has to earn their trust in 3 seconds. Make it count — the difference between a good design and a forgettable one is whether it makes someone feel protected. Push hard.
`,
  relevantFiles: ["src/App.tsx", "src/index.css", "src/main.tsx", "package.json", "tailwind.config.js", "vite.config.ts"]
});
console.log("Design subagent launched");

arguments.content:
# VaultAI — Local Legal Intelligence Platform

## Overview
VaultAI is a local-first legal risk engine for India. Users upload financial and legal documents; an on-device AI scans for hidden risks and delivers a plain-language verdict in 30 seconds. No documents leave the user's device.

## Target Personas
1. **Middle Class** — Tax overpayment, hidden loan charges, insurance mis-selling
2. **Farmer** — Land title verification, fake POA detection, encumbrance checks
3. **Remote Worker** — Cross-border tax compliance, FEMA, contractor misclassification
4. **Freelancer** — Contract forgery detection, IP-transfer overreach, missing milestones

## Tech Stack
- React 18 + TypeScript
- Vite (port 5000)
- Tailwind CSS (with CSS variable color system)
- Framer Motion (animations)
- Lucide React (icons)
- React Router v6

## Project Structure
```
src/
  pages/
    Landing.tsx       — Hero + persona cards + product positioning
    Vault.tsx         — Document management hub
    Analyze.tsx       — Step-by-step analysis demo (per persona)
    HowItWorks.tsx    — Architecture explainer + trust building
  components/
    Navbar.tsx
    Footer.tsx
    RiskBadge.tsx     — Green/amber/red risk indicator
  App.tsx             — Router setup
  index.css           — Design tokens (CSS variables) + base styles
```

## Routes
- `/` — Landing page
- `/vault` — My Secure Vault (document management)
- `/analyze/:persona` — Analysis demo (tax-loan, land-title, cross-border-tax, contract-verify)
- `/how-it-works` — Architecture and privacy explainer

## Design System
- **Primary color**: Deep legal green (`#0b3b2d`)
- **Accent**: Muted gold (`#c5a880`)
- **Risk colors**: Green (safe), Amber (caution), Red (danger)
- **Fonts**: Cabinet Grotesk (headings) + Plus Jakarta Sans (body)

## Running
```bash
npm run dev
```

## User Preferences
- India-specific content throughout (rupee amounts, Indian banks, Indian laws like 80C, FEMA, DTAA)
- No emojis in UI
- Privacy-first positioning (local processing, no cloud upload)

VaultAI is live and fully working. Here's what was built:

**4 pages, all functional:**

- **Landing** (`/`) — Hero with "Before you sign, know what it costs." positioning, trust indicators, persona cards with real India-specific verdicts
- **Vault** (`/vault`) — Document hub with mock files (HDFC home loan flagged Caution, Bangalore plot flagged Danger), health score, upload area, category breakdown
- **Analysis** (`/analyze/tax-loan`, `/land-title`, `/cross-border-tax`, `/contract-verify`) — Animated step-by-step pipeline showing local OCR → rule engine → AI reasoning → risk verdict
- **How It Works** (`/how-it-works`) — Architecture explainer building privacy trust

**Design choices:**
- Deep legal green (`#0b3b2d`) palette — authoritative, not cold
- Cabinet Grotesk + Plus Jakarta Sans — sharp and trustworthy
- Green/Amber/Red risk badge system used consistently everywhere
- "100% Local Processing. Documents never leave your device." present throughout

**Next, I can add a real document upload flow that extracts text client-side using PDF.js and runs pattern-matching rules in the browser — giving users a genuinely functional 30-second risk scan without any backend. Would you like me to do that?**
